blocks.text: Every investment risk on the SIE sorts into two buckets. Systematic risks hit the whole market — you cannot diversify them away. Unsystematic (non-systematic) risks are specific to one company or industry — diversification is exactly the cure. This section covers the big systematic three.

blocks.definition: The risk that prices fall across the entire market, dragging even good companies down. Measured by beta: a beta of 1.0 moves with the market; 1.5 swings half again as hard; 0.5 is calmer. Diversifying among stocks doesn't help — they all fall together. Hedging (index puts) and asset allocation are the tools that do.

blocks.title: Example — beta
blocks.text: The market drops 10%. A beta-1.5 growth stock is expected to drop about 15%; a beta-0.6 utility about 6%. A short position flips the sign — negative-beta assets (rare) rise when the market falls. Beta measures only MARKET-related movement; a drug trial failure is not in beta.

blocks.definition: The risk that rising rates push existing bond prices down. Hits hardest: long maturities and low coupons (remember Chapter 6 — the 30-year zero is the extreme case). Preferred stock and utility shares feel it too, because their fixed payouts compete with bond yields.

The risk that returns fail to outpace rising prices. The quiet killer of 'safe' investments: fixed-income and cash are most exposed; equities and real assets historically defend best; TIPS are the direct antidote. Real return = nominal return − inflation.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A retiree holds a 30-year bond paying $40 per year. With 3% inflation, that $40 buys the equivalent of about $30 in a decade — no default, no missed payment, yet real income quietly shrank 25%. The 'safest' portfolio choice carried the biggest inflation risk.

A fourth systematic cousin appears occasionally: event risk in the broad sense of wars, pandemics, and natural disasters that hit everything at once. The response is the same as market risk — allocation and hedging, not more stocks.

'Which risk CANNOT be diversified away?' — any of market, interest-rate, or inflation risk. If the recommended fix is diversification, the question is about UNsystematic risk (business, credit, concentration). Long-term Treasuries have no credit risk but plenty of interest-rate and inflation risk — 'risk-free' never means risk-free.

blocks.definition: The risk an issuer fails to pay interest or principal. Measured by ratings: BBB−/Baa3 and above is investment grade; below is high-yield ('junk'). Treasuries have essentially none; low-rated corporates and some munis have plenty. Downgrade risk is the milder version: the rating falls, the price follows, even without default.

blocks.text: Credit risk is priced: the lower the rating, the higher the yield an issuer must pay. That's the risk-reward trade in its purest form — and why a portfolio of junk bonds is not an 'income' recommendation for a conservative retiree, whatever the yield. Watch rating boundaries: BBB− (S&P/Fitch) and Baa3 (Moody's) are the last investment-grade rungs; BB+/Ba1 starts junk.

blocks.definition: The risk you can't sell quickly at a fair price. High: DPPs, non-traded REITs, thinly traded small-caps, some munis, structured notes, hedge funds. Low: Treasuries, listed large-cap stocks, ETFs. The classic symptom: a wide bid-ask spread.

The risk of losing the money invested itself — without regard to inflation or income. Options buyers face it totally (a premium can go to zero); so do holders of unproven ventures. Mitigate by limiting position sizes and using guarantees or protective options where they exist.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: An investor puts $3,000 into call options (capital risk: can expire worthless), $50,000 into a non-traded REIT (liquidity risk: no exit for years), and $20,000 into BB-rated bonds yielding 8% (credit risk: that extra yield is default compensation). Three different risks — none of them 'market risk.'

Related flavors worth recognizing: business risk (a single company's management or product fails — unsystematic, diversifiable), financial risk (a company borrowed too much — its leverage magnifies trouble), and regulatory/legislative risk (a rule or tax-law change guts an industry, e.g., a tax-code change hitting munis). Call risk from Chapter 6 belongs in this bucket list too: the issuer takes your best bond away when rates fall.

Match the tell: 'rating downgrade' = credit risk. 'Wide spread / can't find a buyer' = liquidity risk. 'Could lose entire premium' = capital risk. 'One company's poor management' = business risk — the diversifiable one. 'Heavy debt load' = financial risk.

blocks.definition: The risk that instability, expropriation, capital controls, or regime change destroys an investment's value — highest in emerging markets. Distinct from regulatory risk (rule changes in a stable system) and from legislative risk (tax-law changes).

The risk that foreign-currency moves erode dollar returns. Any international holding carries it — including ADRs, even though they trade in dollars (Chapter 5). A weakening foreign currency shrinks both the investment's dollar value and its dividends. Hedging with currency futures or options can offset it — at a cost.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A U.S. investor buys a Japanese stock that gains 10% in yen. Over the same year the yen falls 15% against the dollar. Her dollar return is negative — the stock did its job; the currency took it back.

blocks.definition: The risk that cash flows — coupons, called principal, maturing bonds, mortgage prepayments — must be reinvested at LOWER rates. Bites when rates fall; callable, high-coupon, and mortgage-backed bonds carry the most. Zero-coupon bonds are the escape: nothing to reinvest until maturity.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A retiree's 7% bond is called when rates hit 4.5%. Her $1,000 principal comes back — and the best new bond pays $45 where she earned $70. That gap is reinvestment risk realized. Had she owned a zero maturing the year she needed the money, there'd have been no coupons to reinvest at all.

Notice the mirror: interest-rate risk hurts when rates RISE (prices fall); reinvestment risk hurts when rates FALL (income shrinks). A laddered portfolio — maturities spread across years — splits the difference: each rung matures and reinvests at whatever rates then prevail, averaging the ride.
Zero-coupon bonds: MAXIMUM interest-rate (price) risk, ZERO reinvestment risk. The exam loves that pairing. And ADR questions about risk are currency-risk questions in disguise.

blocks.definition: Too much of a portfolio in one stock, sector, or asset class — so a single event does outsized damage. The classic case: an employee holding most of her 401(k) in employer stock, tying her savings AND her paycheck to one company.

blocks.text: Diversification is the remedy for every unsystematic risk: spread holdings across companies, industries, asset classes, and geographies so no single failure is fatal. Correlation is the key word — diversification works best when assets DON'T move together. Twenty tech stocks is one bet, not twenty. Stocks and bonds pair well precisely because their returns often diverge; adding international and real assets diversifies further.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: Portfolio A: $200,000, all in one regional bank's stock. Portfolio B: $200,000 across an S&P 500 fund, a bond fund, an international fund, and a REIT. A banking scandal cuts the bank's shares 60% — Portfolio A loses $120,000; Portfolio B barely notices. Both still fell in the 2022-style broad selloff, though: diversification never removes MARKET risk.
Building the remedy: allocation and rebalancing

Practical diversifiers the exam rewards: mutual funds and ETFs (instant diversification in one purchase), asset allocation across stocks/bonds/cash tuned to time horizon and risk tolerance, and rebalancing back to targets so winners don't quietly become concentrations. Longer horizons justify more equities (time to recover); short horizons and income needs argue for bonds and cash. Dollar-cost averaging — investing a fixed amount on a schedule — buys more shares when prices are low and produces an average cost per share below the average price.

blocks.caption: Risk vs remedy — the master table

blocks.text: Diversification cures unsystematic risk and does NOTHING for systematic risk. If a question shows a portfolio of one employer's stock, the answer is concentration risk — and the fix is diversify, whatever the employee's loyalty. If it shows a diversified portfolio still falling in a crash, that's market risk working as designed.
